confidence.disclosure: Mimar Koca Sinan, the great Ottoman architect, is well documented through his three autobiographical accounts (Tezkiretü'l-Bünyan, Tezkiretü'l-Ebniye, and Tuhfetü'l-Mi'marin), all dictated to his friend Mustafa Sa'i Çelebi in his old age. From these and from the imperial archives we know roughly when his major buildings were completed and the dates of the campaigns he served on as a young military engineer. The early part of his life - his enrolment in the devshirme around 1512, his service in the Janissary corps, and his rise to chief imperial architect in 1538 - is firm by year. The exact date of his birth is not preserved; only the year of his death is firmly fixed.

sources: Sa'i Mustafa Çelebi, Tezkiretü'l-Bünyan and Tezkiretü'l-Ebniye
Gulru Necipoglu, The Age of Sinan (2005)
Aptullah Kuran, Sinan: The Grand Old Master of Ottoman Architecture (1987)

journey.event: Born around 1489 in the village of Agirnas near Kayseri in central Anatolia, into a Christian (most probably Orthodox Greek or Armenian) family. His father is variously named in the sources as Abdulmennan or Christo. The exact date of his birth is not recorded.

Around 1512 (during the reign of Selim I) is recruited through the devshirme, the Ottoman levy of Christian boys for the imperial slave-soldier corps. Taken to Istanbul, converted to Islam, and trained for the Janissary corps. He is unusually old for a devshirme recruit; the standard age was 8-18.

Serves as a young soldier in Sultan Selim I's eastern campaign against the Safavid Shah Isma'il I. On 23 August 1514 fights at the Battle of Chaldiran, the great Ottoman victory over the Safavids that saves eastern Anatolia for the Sunni Ottomans.

Takes part in Selim I's Egyptian campaign that destroys the Mamluk Sultanate (1516-1517). Selim's victory at the Battle of Ridaniyya outside Cairo on 22 January 1517 brings Egypt, Syria, the Hijaz, and the holy cities of Mecca and Medina under Ottoman rule. Sinan sees the great architecture of Mamluk Cairo for the first time.

Under the new sultan Suleiman I, takes part in the conquest of Belgrade in August 1521 - a goal Mehmed II had failed to achieve in 1456. By this time Sinan has been promoted within the Janissary corps and serves as a military engineer.

Takes part in the long siege of Rhodes in 1522, which finally falls to the Ottomans in December after a six-month siege. The Knights Hospitaller surrender and are allowed to evacuate to Malta. Sinan is by now a recognised military engineer.
On 29 August 1526 fights at the Battle of Mohacs, where the Ottomans annihilate the Hungarian army and kill King Louis II of Hungary. The battle opens central Europe to Ottoman power.

Serves at the first Ottoman siege of Vienna in September-October 1529. The siege is unsuccessful (the season is too late and the artillery train cannot be brought up), and Suleiman withdraws. Sinan demonstrates his skill at building bridges and siege works during the campaign.

Serves on the eastern campaign against the Safavids in 1534-1535. As military engineer he constructs ships on Lake Van for an attempted naval crossing - a feat that brings him to the personal notice of the sultan and the grand vizier Ibrahim Pasha. Soon afterward he is promoted to the head of the corps of military engineers.

In 1538, on the death of the previous chief court architect Acem Esir Ali, Sinan is appointed Mimarbasi (chief imperial architect). He will hold this position for the next 50 years, until his death in 1588. By his own count he will design and supervise the construction of about 477 buildings - mosques, madrasas, hospitals, bridges, baths, mausoleums, palaces, aqueducts.

Completes the Sehzade Mosque in Istanbul in 1548, his first major imperial mosque, built for Sultan Suleiman in memory of his deceased son Sehzade Mehmed. Sinan calls it the work of his apprenticeship. Its symmetrical four-half-dome plan is a major innovation.

Completes the Süleymaniye Mosque in Istanbul in 1557, after seven years of construction. This is the great imperial mosque of Suleiman I, set on the third hill of the city overlooking the Golden Horn. Sinan calls it the work of his journeymanship. The dome is 53 metres above the floor and 26.5 metres in diameter, comparable to that of Hagia Sophia.

Completes the Selimiye Mosque in Edirne in 1574-1575, after seven years of construction. Built for Sultan Selim II, this is Sinan's masterpiece - the building he calls the work of his mastery. The dome is 31.28 metres in diameter (slightly larger than that of Hagia Sophia) and rests on eight massive piers in an octagonal arrangement that opens up the interior space. He completes it at the age of 86.

In his last decade Sinan dictates his autobiographical accounts to his friend Sa'i Mustafa Çelebi. He continues to supervise building projects and to receive imperial commissions even past the age of 90. His list of completed works in the Tezkiretü'l-Ebniye includes 92 mosques, 52 small mosques, 55 madrasas, 7 schools, 20 mausoleums, and many other buildings.

Dies on 17 July 1588 in Istanbul, aged about 99 (a remarkable age for the period). He is buried in a small tomb of his own design just outside the wall of the Suleymaniye Mosque complex, where he had built a house for himself in the years of his mastership. The tomb is still there. His pupils carry his style across the Ottoman empire and far beyond, into the Mughal architecture of India.
